sentences.text: They didn't count anything.
Was not normal, because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They didn't count.
I was-- when I was there--
In the daytime, they sent me to work.
And at night, we were staying in the barracks from the soldiers, from the army, the Polish.
A couple of days later, I find out all the family, the [INAUDIBLE], the women and the children, they are in Miedzyrzec.
I sneaked out from that place Friday night-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --and I went to Miedzyrzec.
I walked.
I walked.
26 kilometers, me and another couple guys.
I went there.
And I came and I saw my sister was-- my mother-- I find it out my mother committed suicide.
Our neighbor from the same apartment, in the same building, he was [? hiding himself ?] into the steps.
And he heard when a Polish-- the mayor went upstairs to see what is going on there, and he said in Polish to the next man to him that the woman committed suicide.
How she commit, what she commit-- because she was unconscious, maybe she woke up in a minute, but she didn't see the family.
Maybe [? killed ?] her.
But I'll go back to this.
In Miedzyrzec, I came in there.
I was there, my two brother-in-laws, my sister-in-law, my sisters.
One sister, they took, was arrested by the Polish police.
And there is no life.
They didn't know what the world's going to be.
But everybody has been saying, why are they keeping us so long?
Let me get finished already.
Because in Miedzyrzec, the originals was already destroyed.
What was in Miedzyrzec?
Tell me.
There was a ghetto where they bring-- there was the concentration camp-- concentrate the people from there to the train and to Treblinka.
We know it already exactly.
I spoke to a man later.
He was in Treblinka, and he was in the gas chamber.
At night-- I mixed them up.
Maybe it's not right.
But this is-- I don't want to lose this.
He was-- they took him from Miedzyrzec, and he was in the gas chamber.
And at night, that time, there was no crematorium.
It was a ditch.
They dumped them in, all of them, and they burned them.
But that particular day, it was late already.
They didn't burn.
And he was laying within the bodies.
And he was probably strong-- [INAUDIBLE] strong guards.
Then he said that the atmosphere, the air, took out from him, the gas, and he woke up and he looked around.
Then he came to conscious and he left.
He told me the whole story.
He was there.
His name was Rabinowitz.
No.
I didn't see him anymore.
I saw him that time, and he explained to me everything, how it was, the whole thing to what happened.
In Miedzyrzec.
I came to Miedzyrzec.
I see [INAUDIBLE] laying on the floors.
They don't have--
There was no time-- it was, in Biala, 10,000 people.
They sent about 12,000 people-- 5,000.
The rest they took--
To Miedzyrzec, 4,000 or 5,000 people from Biala.
In Miedzyrzec were 4,000 or 5,000.
Yeah.
But Miedzyrzec was already cleaned up.
They was, in Treblinka, killed already.
Yeah.
Then, I looked around, and I was for two days in [INAUDIBLE].
They shoot and they killed my sister.
When the-- in [INAUDIBLE].
No.
I know.
I was-- one niece saw it all.
On the cemetery-- the Jewish cemetery in Miedzyrzec.
There, she was with maybe 200 people in one of the ditch.
The shooting.
They tell them to lay down, ta-ta-ta-ta-ta, and that's it.
I was-- till Saturday night-- --till Sunday night.
Sunday was Simchat Torah.
And all of them said to my family, go back.
Maybe you will survive.
Go back to Biala.
And I had to go, because I see it was-- maybe, maybe-- they talked me into it.
I left Miedzyrzec and I went back to Biala.
Walked, 26 kilometers, at night.
All night.
So what happened?
When I arrived in Biala in the morning, I just made it to go into the barracks before the daylight.
I waited two days later.
They took all-- I was working in Biala in the city, building barracks for the Wehrmacht.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --a boy came.
He went with a German to the-- to the-- to pick up some wood.
And he came back.
The boy maybe was 15 years old.
He said, I saw the old-- the old people from the barracks from the army-- this was the 34th army [INAUDIBLE].
The Polish army, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I saw them go with the packages to the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Then, a minute later, [INAUDIBLE], a German, came [INAUDIBLE] with the loudspeaker, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And I was away in the back.
Then my cousin and my friend asked me, what are we going to do?
I said disappear.
I had a loaf of bread, a big one, the Polish, maybe 8-- 20 pounds, in my jacket.
I throw it away.
[WHISTLES]
And I disappeared.
Where did you go to?
Oh, now where are you going to go?
So I built with goyim and I built with gendarmes and I built with [INAUDIBLE].
But I didn't have a choice.
I had to go.
And I-- --and I tried to go with the side streets whenever I could.
No, I went to my small-- it's my hometown.
Konstantynow But it was too early.
This was right-- a week after Yom Kippur-- after Sukkot.
The first night Sukkot was-- the first night Sukkot [? was the execution, ?] all of Biala.
I have this on the tape there, the date.
So what happened?
I came--
I went to Konstantynow.
It was 18 kilometers.
And before, I couldn't go in.
It was still the daylight.
I went in the woods, wait till it get dark.
And I feel like I'm getting fainted, because I was with the [? impact ?] running, and scared.
I was-- I was afraid for a little thing.
And I was running with the [? impact. ?] Then I came in.
I see going out.
But I'm talking to myself, stay on your feet.
This is not the time.
Not weak.
I was not tired.
I was just exhausted from everything.
And at night, I went into the town.
And I went into-- me and the two with me, they went in this other place.
I told them where to go.
And I went in the city, outskirts, in Konstantynow, and I told them, tomorrow morning, let me go to Miedzyrzec, see what's going on with the Jews.
No.
Was no house, no.
I ran into a Polish guy.
I picked up one of the best.
Yeah, a Polish farmer.
His name was [?
Waslaw ?] [?
Dubinsky. ?] Then I said, [?
Waslaw ?] tomorrow, you go to Miedzyrzec-- to [?
Lodz, ?] because in [?
Lodz ?] was a small ghetto.
I figure, if they're still alive, if they're still not touched in Miedzyrzec, I'll take them to-- [?
Lodz. ?] It was a small ghetto.
It was ?] everybody destroyed.
Was a few Jews to clean the ghetto.
So what happened?
And I said, go get me the other guy, a guy from-- one I sent to [?
Lodz ?] and one to Miedzyrzec by bicycle.
And I wrote letters to them, what I know.
I was waiting all day on the [INAUDIBLE].
And at the evening-- in the late afternoon they came home.
[INAUDIBLE] told me Miedzyrzec-- Lodz, you could come.
I had a letter sent me back.
You could come.
How long they're going to be, I don't know.
Went up to Miedzyrzec.
He came back.
He said, Louis, nobody is there.
Jewish blood is up to the knees.
They cleaned them up yesterday.
That day when I ran away, now they cleaned them up.
What happened?
I find it out a couple of days later.
They took the people from that-- Miedzyrzec
Barracks, from the barracks-- to the train.
They pushed into 250 people in a wagon.
They pushed a [? bench, ?] and they knocked them.
And one of the other was hanging.
[INAUDIBLE] shot one down, the other eyes, ears, the nose, the hair.
It was terrible.
These wagons was terrible.
You know what they did, the Germans?
From Miedzyrzec.
This is from Biala.
The man-- the man-- this was from Biala.
They pushed them in in the wagons to 250, maybe more.
And they beat them up, and one was hanging on the other one.
They [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE].
They send the train to Russia.
To Russia.
And on the way, about 20 miles away, they turned around the train and brought them to Miedzyrzec.
And this was the women with the children, with their people was forced from Biala.
That time, I would say maybe 20,000 Jews was in that train.
The train was pressing.
That night, it went out all the way to Treblinka.
A friend of mine jumped out from the train.
He came to Konstantynow.
Not in Konstantynow, in the neighborhood, in [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, you got it.
I got every date.
Every date.
In 1942, this was-- this was two-- two days after Simchat Torah.
Yeah.
Motel Goldring.
He's still alive.
He's in Israel.
My friend, my [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
To me?
Then I went-- when he came back and told me the story, then I had nowhere to go.
Then I'm sitting with two friends-- my cousin and my friend [INAUDIBLE].
This was Wednesday.
I came to them Wednesday night, tell them the story what-- the message, what I got from Miedzyrzec to that Polack [INAUDIBLE] from [?
Lodz. ?]
So what happened?
I was sitting there, and we cried.
We didn't know what to do.
We cannot sit here.
We can not-- what we're going to do?
This was Saturday night.
One boy, my cousin's boy, [INAUDIBLE], 10 years old.
He came from Miedzyrzec.
He was hiding himself in a cellar.
And the grandpa was in cellar.
After they [INAUDIBLE], the grandpa took that grandson, and they came to-- in our direction, in our neighborhood.
And he came to that farmer, and he told me, your sister, your brother-in-law, two-- and two nieces, they are alive in that basement.
Because I was a couple days before in Miedzyrzec.
And he told me this.
Then I picked up myself, and I went in the daytime to Miedzyrzec.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way, I hired a man, what he took me by horse and wagon.
Unfortunately, I went through in a fire.
I ran in a fire in a terrible spot.
It was a [INAUDIBLE].
They [? were wanted for ?] Russian prisoners, the Gestapo.
But somehow, I sneaked through.
I came into Miedzyrzec, and I came into my basement.
There it was.
And I saw them.
They were so crying.
They were so happy.
They had been thinking, [INAUDIBLE], God came and gave us life.
All right.
I got-- right away, I organized water for them, bread.
And we were there a couple of days.
The third day, they grabbed me to work.
They still was sitting in attics.
I went out to get something for them.
They grabbed me to work.
What happened?
What kind of work?
From the neighborhood city-- from [?
Rzeczyca. ?] Rzeczyca was the capital for Rzeczyca.
But in Miedzyrzec was concentrating all of them from the whole area.
I'm telling you, 50,000 people were there.
More than 5,000 children was killed from Miedzyrzec.
So what happened?
A volksdeutsche took a loudspeaker and went out in ghetto, in Rzeczyca, and he said, people in the Jewish, come out.
Don't be afraid.
There is no anymore [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and no more transports.
Come out, and you're going to be-- you go to Miedzyrzec, and there is a Judenstaat.
The people was ready now, for weeks in the cellars, and [INAUDIBLE] in the bunkers there.
We went out.
We came to Miedzyrzec.
The minute they came to Miedzyrzec, [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE], they were [? staged and ?] shooting them, right away on the spot.
And they take me.
I should take me and another boy to the cemetery, to the Jewish.
I was-- finally, my [INAUDIBLE] was with Jewish blood.
And every-- dead bodies are so heavy, you couldn't lift it, the two of us.
I was thinking they would do me a favor, they would kill me.
Why do I have to do this, with my brothers' and sisters' Jewish life so destroyed?
But I didn't have a choice.
I brought them to the cemetery, on the Jewish, and I take a little-- was people working there, like in a slaughterhouse, blood all over.
And my clothes was with blood.
By Jewish law, my clothes should be taken off and buried, because Jewish blood cannot be [INAUDIBLE].
I came back to my group, and I was so-- I went in a shock.
I didn't have water to wash my hands.
And I was-- I lost myself.
This is not the same Louis.
What happened?
I had a habit to talk to myself.
Hey, you got to stay on your feet.
You're fighting.
Keep fighting.
Was another few days, they opened up the ghetto.
Then we came out.
There was no place better to go.
The Judenrat.
The police-- the Gestapo, they said they should open up.
And then we start to look-- it was [INAUDIBLE] we were finding-- [INAUDIBLE] each other.
All right.
I was with them.
And a couple of weeks later, I took-- a short while later, again, an execution.
In Miedzyrzec
This was already-- one, two-- this was the third transport.
They had seven transports there.
Six transports-- seven.
What happened?
The people came in from Rzeczyca, from all over, from the whole area.
And there was--
After the transport, they opened up the ghetto again.
They knew already there was people [? hiding ?] themselves.
They know there's at least that many people.
Always asking for-- for a list, how many population.
So they took so much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they opened up the ghetto, they find each other.
They saw a few from my town, a few from the other town.
They was looking to get some place.
We was staying-- we find a room.
What can I tell you-- I'll tell you the dimensions.
It was probably-- --8 foot by 20.
We was 13 people in that room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a couple of days later, again a transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- but before the transport, I was hiding myself in a cellar.
They caught me.
They brought me in to--
--to a room, to arrest.
It was a few people there, about 10, 15 they caught.
They're going to be-- what happened?
Example, they caught me before lunch probably-- yes.
Then-- When did you--
This was a week later, after the first transport, the 20,000 people.
I was not in the transport.
They didn't come in.
You mean that for Rzeczyca?
I was-- they took me right away, before the transport arrived.
They had [? grabbed ?] me and another guy.
Tag-tag-tag-tag, tag-tag-tag-tag, that's It.
It was a few horse and wagons.
They sent them to Treblinka.
I'll tell you.
Wait one minute.
This was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --yeah.
[SIGHS]
That time, when they caught me, they took me in arrest.
It was about 15, 20 people.
This was in the morning, before lunch probably.
And what happened?
By sunset came [NON-ENGLISH] with a few Polish police, [INAUDIBLE] Ukrainian.
They came in.
They brought us down from the upstairs to the downstairs.
From the attic?
From the attic, yeah.
No, they was in a room.
Not a legal apartment, but a home [INAUDIBLE].
He said, [NON-ENGLISH], be honest.
Give up what you have, gold, money, division.
Tomorrow, you're going to work.
You get $3 a day.
And I know this is the biggest lie.
They were gelt, gold division.
That was zlotys.
Gold-- gelt-- gelt is the zlotys in division-- dollars.
What happened?
Everybody would give away.
He start to shoot them right away.
He recognized one guy.
Oh, you're still alive, you [? runt? ?] Right away, he shot him.
[INAUDIBLE] boy [NON-ENGLISH].
And you know what happened?
He told me-- they ordered me to get naked.
He was checking I had money here in the [NON-ENGLISH].
I was shaking.
Me and my sister-in-law was there, and I didn't want to be killed.
She should see.
Maybe she would survive [INAUDIBLE].
Not to-- [INAUDIBLE].
I just-- you start a second, a second.
[NON-ENGLISH] I threw it into the desk.
The money.
And they checked.
They see us from a lighting--
I had a stone from a lighter.
Oh, a diamond.
No, this is in the lighter, [NON-ENGLISH] They brought us into the shul-- to the synagogue.
In the synagogue, [NON-ENGLISH] was up to here in [INAUDIBLE] boots.
Was crying, cursing.
And this was-- the shul was the nicest in the area from 100 miles around.
And they took me.
And I was-- and I went upstairs in the women's section.
This was maybe about 35 feet.
And my sister-in-law, she started to complain.
She has a gallstone attack.
I took off my jacket and I put her away.
And I was sitting-- and I was trying to-- and I told her.
If I'll jump out, try to jump.
Don't wait.
Go there and there and there and there, and maybe we will see each other.
From the shul.
What happened?
I was going around, and we cannot go out.
The windows is blockaded from the Gestapo, from the gendarmerie, and they will shoot [? around, ?] and you can't [? run ?].
It was already late in the night, probably, maybe around 11, 12 o'clock, and I went there.
And I was sitting near the [? aisle. ?] And I tried to help her, but she is cursing.
She is in terrible pain.
Who knows what means gallstone attack?
A doctor maybe knows.
Who went through this?
It was late at night, around 1 o'clock, maybe 2 o'clock.
I was dreaming.
Of what?
I don't know.
My father came in.
Louis, you came here to sleep.
This is not that place.
Go.
I woke up.
I lift up myself [? with impact ?] And I went to a window, and I was there five times.
I couldn't do nothing.
This time, I came to the window.
[INAUDIBLE] a woman with two boys.
They're frightened.
She wants to jump.
She had a couple towels that made a [INAUDIBLE] strong, something to catch.
She wants to jump.
They wouldn't let her.
They [INAUDIBLE].
And they've-- are bargaining between each other.
And this woman was with me already when the police arrested.
Then she said to me, you want to jump?
Jump.
I said, yes.
And I said, tell my sister-in-law-- she knows her-- to do the same thing what I did.
I went and I took the cut piece of cloth, and I looked--
[WHISTLES] --jumped down, took off my shoes-- the boots, [INAUDIBLE], and I went in.
There was destroyed Jewish houses already.
I went into a window.
I went out the door.
The shooting was after me.
And I went outside from the city.
I was sitting there, and I was thinking what to do.
What can I have-- in ghetto-- outside of the ghetto was [INAUDIBLE], my sister-in-law-- no, my niece, my nephew-- three nieces and one nephew.
What shall I do?
I don't know.
Go back to them?
I have no strength.
I have no guts.
Go further?
I cannot leave them.
But I make my decision.
I went in the direction to Konstantynow.
I-- Which is which direction?
Towards them?
And I left them on the [INAUDIBLE], and I went walking there.
And this is 36 kilometers.
Huh?
She said-- she told them, Louis jumped.
She said, let him succeed.
I am not going.
I'm going to Treblinka, [NON-ENGLISH].
This was the answer.
All right.
Go ahead.
